Goal is to preserve space while creating merges and keeping larger tiles positioned stably. Checking moves: Up gives columns [0,2,0,4]->[2,4,0,0], [0,0,4,32]->[4,32,0,0], [0,32,64,8] unchanged, [4,8,4,2] unchanged; no merges, limited improvement. Down similarly gives no merges. Right gives row1 unchanged, row2 [2,0,32,8]->[0,2,32,8], row3 [0,4,64,4]->[0,4,64,4], row4 unchanged; no merges. Left gives row1 [0,0,0,4]->[4,0,0,0], row2 [2,0,32,8]->[2,32,8,0], row3 [0,4,64,4]->[4,64,4,0], row4 [4,32,8,2] unchanged; no merges but consolidates tiles to the left, increases monotonic structure, and opens a full empty rightmost column except bottom. Best sub-goal is improve board shape and maintain mobility. Left best supports future merges and space.

Evaluating moves: left changes top row to [8,2,0,0] but no merges elsewhere; right changes top row to [0,0,8,2], also no merges. Down appears invalid because every column is blocked with no movement. Up is best because column 4 has [2,2,16,2] which merges the top two 2s into 4, creating an extra empty space and improving mobility while keeping the board alive. Sub-goal now is maximizing open cells and legal future moves, so choose up.

Immediate merges available if moving left on top row (16+16) and likely preserves empties, while moving right also merges top row but shifts large tiles away. Best sub-goal now is safe value gain plus board simplification.

Highest tile 128 is at row 2, col 2, with 64 below it and relatively large values concentrated toward the lower-right. Goal: preserve/open space while keeping larger tiles clustered and enabling merges, especially the three 2s in the rightmost column. Candidate actions: Right would merge the two top-row 2s and the two lower-row 2s in the right column while keeping big tiles on the right side and likely increasing empty spaces. Down keeps high tiles low but does not create the immediate double merge and may disturb structure less favorably. Left shifts high tiles away from the right-side cluster. Up pulls the 64/128 upward, weakening the bottom-heavy setup. Best sub-goal now is immediate safe merges plus more space while maintaining strong tile positioning. Therefore choose right.

Checking actions: left appears to make no row merges or shifts, so likely invalid. Down also seems to leave every column unchanged, likely invalid. experiences.intentions: Board is full, so I need a move that both is legal and ideally creates space via merges while preserving the 128 tile. Check rows/columns: left gives merges in row1 (4+4) and row4 (2+2); right also gives two merges; up gives only one merge in col1; down gives one merge in col4. My sub-goal is maximize immediate space creation. Between left and right, left keeps larger tiles more centered/left and avoids pushing the 128 to the edge, so it is the best move now.

experiences.intentions: Key state: the 4x4 board is full, so only moves that create merges are legal/useful. Existing merge opportunities are in the top row [8,32,2,2], where the two 2s can merge if moved left or right, and in the first column [8,2,4,2] none, second [32,32,8,64] can merge if moved up/down, others mostly none. Goal: make a legal move that opens space while preserving the strong 128 tile and keeping higher tiles reasonably grouped. Candidate actions: left merges the top-row 2s into a 4 and also keeps 32s in column 2 aligned; right also merges top-row 2s but shifts the 128 from column 3 to column 4, which is less stable. Up merges the two 32s in column 2 into 64 but moves many tiles upward and separates structure around 128. Down also merges the 32s but drags the 128 toward the bottom, disrupting position more. Best sub-goal is to create space with minimal disruption, so left is preferred.

experiences.intentions: Board is full, so only actions that create merges are legal/progress-making. Checking rows/columns: left/right give no adjacent equal tiles in any row, so likely no movement. Up/down each have one merge in column 0: [8,2,4,2] no, column1 [32,32,8,64] has a merge at top if moved up/down, actually only the two 32s can combine. Moving up merges them near the top and keeps the 128 tile in place; moving down also merges but shifts more tiles downward. Goal is to preserve board structure and create space with minimal disruption, so pursue the available merge while keeping the highest tile stable. Up is best.
